title: fetchLargeFixture has no cache, causing repeated network fetch and SHA-256 verification on every call
description: loadFixture (line 42-92) stores its promise in a module-level Map so concurrent and repeat callers share one fetch. fetchLargeFixture has no equivalent cache: every call issues a new network request and then re-runs the expensive crypto.subtle.digest verify pass over the full payload. Large fixtures are exactly the ones where re-fetching is most costly. Adding the same promise-cache pattern used by loadFixture would eliminate redundant I/O and CPU on repeated calls in the same process lifetime.

title: verifySha256 allocates an intermediate plain JS Array and 32 short strings to build the hex digest
description: Array.from(new Uint8Array(digestBuffer)) at line 34 copies the 32-byte digest into a plain JS Array (heap allocation), then .map(b => b.toString(16).padStart(2, '0')) creates 32 short string objects, and finally .join('') concatenates them. This is called on every non-cached fixture load. A single manual loop writing into a pre-sized string or using a DataView would avoid the intermediate array and the 32 per-byte string allocations. With only 10 fixtures in the corpus the absolute cost is negligible, but the pattern is unnecessarily wasteful for a crypto verify utility that could be called in batch test runs.

title: encodeJxl assembles chunk output with two separate iteration passes over the chunks array
description: After collecting encoder output chunks (line 22-25), the function first iterates all chunks to sum their lengths (lines 33-34) and then iterates again to copy them into the output Uint8Array (lines 36-40). A single pass using a growing typed array or a Blob constructor would halve the iteration count. Although this runs only at fixture-generation time on small 100×100 images where the total chunk count is tiny, the two-pass pattern is a style issue that could become a real cost if larger fixtures are added.

title: downscaleRgba recomputes floating-point source-pixel bounds inside the inner-most loop body
description: In the area-average downscale kernel, syMin/syMax are derived from dy (outer loop) and sxMin/sxMax from dx (middle loop). The Math.floor/Math.ceil bounds and yWeight are recomputed fresh for every (dy, dx, sy) triple. Hoisting the sx/sy loop bounds and yWeight out of the inner sx loop (they do not depend on sx) would save two Math.min/Math.max and one subtraction per inner-loop iteration. For a 12MP RAW downscaled to 256px this inner loop executes ~46M times, making this a meaningful saving in a one-time script that is already identified as a cost center.

title: No benchmark or timing guard for corpus fixture load time; regressions in verifySha256 or fetch path would be invisible in CI
description: The loader is the hot path for every test suite that calls loadFixture in a loop across all 10 fixtures. There is no timing assertion or benchmark script. A slow crypto.subtle implementation or an accidental synchronous stall would not surface in the existing tests (loader.test.ts only asserts correctness). Adding a simple wall-time check (e.g. all 10 fixtures load in < 500ms) would catch loader regressions early.

const byId = new Map<string, FixtureManifest>(corpusManifest.fixtures.map(f => [f.id, f]));
const cache = new Map<string, Promise<...>>();
